entry.title: VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon 2026: Champions Crowned at SYNC DemoJam
entry.content:
The Stage is Set
The VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon 2026 reached its spectacular grand finale on the main stage of VNSG SYNC 2026 – the annual VNSG and SAP Netherlands year event. In front of a packed keynote audience on 12 March, three finalist teams had exactly five minutes to demonstrate the business impact of their SAP BTP-powered innovations. The audience then cast their votes – and the results were electric.

From Challenge to Champion: The Journey
The hackathon launched in late 2025 when VNSG, SAP BTP Product Management and SAP NL Presales invited Dutch SAP customers and partners to submit real-life business challenges. Six teams were selected and, after a structured enablement phase, spent weeks co-building solutions with dedicated coaches. In February 2026, all six teams pitched to a jury panel, which selected three finalists for the grand DemoJam finale at SYNC. The three finalists stood out for their clear business relevance, technical ambition and the real-world potential to take their prototypes to production.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Champion: Promocean + SOA People — Purchase Order Control Center
Luc van Elk and Thomas Langhout from Promocean, supported by SOA People, took the audience vote and claimed the top spot. Their solution – the Purchase Order Control Center – puts SAP Business AI at the heart of procurement operations.
By connecting AI directly to SAP ECC, the team demonstrated how intelligent automation can dramatically reduce manual effort, accelerate purchase order processing, and surface exceptions before they become costly problems. A showcase of how AI on SAP BTP can deliver operational excellence in one of the most common – and pain-prone – business processes in any enterprise.

Runner-up: Ecotone + Expertum — Integration Suite Management
Arjan de Mol and Bart van de Kamp from Ecotone, partnered with Expertum Consulting, earned second place with a solution that tackles a pain many integration teams know all too well: managing and controlling large numbers of iFlows across landscapes. Their Integration Suite Management solution brings Joule-powered automation to iFlow lifecycle management – enabling operators to undeploy and deploy groups of iFlows intelligently. This turns a time-consuming, error-prone manual process into a governed, AI-assisted workflow, giving integration teams transparency, stability and control. 

Third Place: NTT DATA Business Solutions — ProTrack
Laurens Steffers and Jorrit van der Sanden from NTT DATA Business Solutions presented ProTrack – a solution delivering real-time insight for machines on the production floor. Built on SAP BTP, ProTrack addresses a critical operational need in manufacturing: knowing what is happening on your shop floor right now, not minutes later. By streaming machine data into an SAP BTP-powered dashboard, teams can react faster to exceptions, reduce downtime and optimize throughput.

 

Beyond the Podium: A Strong Field
The jury had a hard job. Aarini Consulting, Nederlandse Spoorwegen (NS), and Simac IT & BSC also built
impressive solutions that demonstrated real business value on SAP BTP. Every team that reached the jury pitch proved
that you can go from business challenge to working prototype in a short time - when you combine the right platform
with the right people. A sincere congratulations to all six participating teams !!

Behind the Scenes
Want to experience the atmosphere of the DemoJam from backstage? Watch the behind-the-scenes video - including a
live demo of how SAP Business AI is changing the way people interact with SAP, presented by Jason Enti and Ronald
Schippers: Behind the scenes at the VNSG SAP BTP DemoJam SYNC 2026

The People Who Made it Happen
A hackathon like this does not happen by itself. A huge thank you to everyone who contributed:
Presented at SYNC by: Twan van den Broek and Irene Rompa
Team coaches: Jan Penninkhof , Alex van den Hoek and Twan van den Broek
Jury: Ronald Schippersm Steven Nuyts, Steffen Schad, Jonathan Freeland
Made possible by: Mientje Paais, Olaf Winkler, Judy Bui and Niklas Siemer

What This Proves
The VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon 2026 is proof that innovation does not require a blank slate or a greenfield project. It requires the right platform, the right people and a real problem worth solving.
SAP BTP – combining SAP Integration Suite, SAP Build, SAP HANA Cloud, SAP Business AI and cloud-native runtimes – gives teams everything they need to move from prototype to production at speed.
The finalist solutions were not demos for the sake of demos. They addressed real procurement pain, real integration
complexity and real manufacturing visibility gaps. And they were built in weeks, not months.

What's Next?
The conversation does not end on the SYNC stage. All teams are encouraged to work with their coaches to plan production rollouts and turn hackathon prototypes into enterprise solutions.

For the broader SAP Community : Stay tuned for the next edition of the VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon. Bring your own
real-life business challenge and co-build with partners and SAP experts.
And who knows - your solution could be on the SYNC main stage in 2027.

SAP Business Technology Platform SAP Build SAP Integration Suite SAP Business AI 

@hansvp @qmrjvd @sricsi98 @BvE @mdschoenmakers @tamasszirtes @f_van_leeuwen @Petra1 @BartvdKamp @tedcastelijns @Arjan_deMol @dvvelzen @JdTeuling @AnnikaHeus @LaurensSteffers

Related reading:
When Innovation Meets Real Business Impact – VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon 2026
Grand Finale VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon in a DemoJam

entry.title: Clean Core for Utilities: The Missing Link Between S/4HANA and AI Driven Operations
entry.content:
Clean Core for Utilities: The Missing Link Between S/4HANA and AI‑Driven Operations

 

Most of the Utilities organization are entering one of the most disruptive decades the industry has ever seen. Aging assets due to modern technologies, rising customer expectations due to digital world, and the rapid growth of renewables are forcing companies to rethink how they operate and manage their system. Many utilities organizations have already started their S/4HANA programs with the hope that modernization will naturally lead to automation, intelligence, and AI-driven insights.

But here’s the reality most teams discover the hard way:

AI doesn’t scale on a messy foundation. And without a Clean Core, S/4HANA alone won’t get you there.

Across utilities in the U.S., APAC, Europe and the Middle East, the organizations making real progress but having  one thing in common: they aren’t just migrating — they’re rebuilding their architectural backbone from scratch of the system or existing design so AI can deliver business value the way they want it to get modern success.

Why Utilities Are Still Struggling

Many utilities organizations are carrying 15–25 years of technical debt. Their ECC  or R3 and IS‑U systems often include:

  • Custom code buried in user exits, BADI, FQ Event and enhancements both implicit and explicit.
  • Hard‑coded logic only a developer or few team member understand.
  • Manual steps scattered across the meter‑to‑cash cycle.
  • Data spread across GIS, AMI, OMS, CIS and legacy and other third-party tools.

The result is a fragmented landscape where every process behaves differently. And that’s the real blocker. Many utilities think their challenge is adopting AI. But the truth is:

AI cannot operate on inconsistent data or unpredictable processes.

What Clean Core Actually Means         

This important point is most of the people misconception that Clean Core means “no customization.” That’s not realistic for utilities, at least for now — regulatory and market needs are too complex, and greater flexibility is still needed, but future may change for above statement is true for now.

Clean Core is not about eliminating logic. It’s about putting logic in the right place.

A practical Clean Core approach means:

1. Keep SAP S/4HANA Utilities standard

Use it as a stable, upgrade‑safe system of record for metering, billing, finance, and core operations.

2. Move differentiation to SAP BTP

Build workflows, extensions, and apps on BTP — not inside the core.

3. Use events instead of modifications

A meter read posted in SAP S/4HANA Utilities should trigger an event. That event can activate workflows, validations, or AI agents on BTP — without touching billing logic.

Clean Core doesn’t slow innovation. It creates the conditions for innovation to scale safely.

Why Clean Core Is the Missing Link for AI

AI only works well when three things are true:

  • Data is consistent, this must have.
  • Processes are predictable, of course this is something to understandable.
  • System behavior is stable; this is bottleneck as well.

Clean Core enforces all three.

Without it, AI stays stuck in pilots’ phase. With it, AI becomes operational. Utilities that adopt Clean Core unlock outcomes which are:

  • Predictive billing to reduce customer call and dispute resolution.
  • Autonomous collections based on customer behavior. Important for utility industries.
  • DER/EV readiness without breaking billing logic, of course main source of revenue generation.
  • Revenue integrity through real‑time anomaly detection.

This shifts from reactive operations to predictive and autonomous ones and trust me this is future of utilities at least from my prospective with AI Era and digital world we are moving in.

Reference Architecture for an AI‑Ready Utility

This future architecture can be understood in three layers:

1.       Transactional Core (SAP S/4HANA Utilities)
  • System of record for metering, billing, and finance data
  • Standardized and upgrade‑friendly
  • Minimal custom code
2.       Innovation Layer (SAP BTP)

Where differentiation lives. Using:

  • Integration Suite
  • Build Process Automation
  • Event Mesh
  • AI Foundation
  • Joule Agents
  • Customer microservices

This is where utilities build intelligent workflows and automation — without touching the core.

3.       AI‑Driven Operations

The outcome layer:

  • Predictive insights
  • Automated processes
  • AI agents managing meter‑to‑cash
  • Intelligent customer interactions
  • Real‑time decisioning

This isn’t just a tech stack — it’s a new operating model.

A Practical Maturity Path: Utilities don’t need to jump to AI overnight. A realistic path looks like which I feel should be:

  • Level 1: Customized Core (Reactive): Heavy custom code, manual processes, limited visibility.
  • Level 2: Clean Core + BTP (Responsive): Standard core with side‑by‑side innovation.
  • Level 3: Event‑Driven + AI (Autonomous): Real‑time processes with AI‑driven decisioning.

The goal isn’t perfection — it’s steady movement toward a scalable, intelligent architecture.

 

 

How to Get Started

Here is practical first steps:

  1. Assess your landscape, identify custom code that can be retired or moved out of the core.
  2. Adopt standard processes first S/4HANA already solves many historical customizations.
  3. Build side‑by‑side by default innovation belongs on BTP.
  4. Prioritize data quality AI is only as good as the data feeding it.

Conclusion

Clean Core is no longer an architectural preference — it’s a prerequisite for AI‑driven utilities.

The future utility isn’t just delivering power or water or gas. It’s managing data, events, and intelligent processes in real time.

Utilities that recognize this shift early won’t just modernize — they’ll redefine how the industry operates.

entry.title: Architecting Electronic Bill of Lading on SAP BTP - End-to-End Scenario(Part 3 of 3)
entry.content:
Introduction

In Part 1 – Case Study and Part 2 – Blueprint, we introduced the concept of the Electronic Bill of Lading and walked through the key steps required for its implementation.

The Part 3 presents a complete end-to-end global trade scenario, beginning with a container loading event emitted by the Terminal Operating System, continuing through the issuance of the electronic Bill of Lading, and ending with the surrender of the eBL when the importer claims the goods.

Teaser

The recently announced trade agreement between the European Union and Mercosur (January 2026) opens new opportunities for smaller producers to gain greater market access and visibility in cross-border commerce. Additionally, the European Union will contribute with €1.8 billion through the Global Gateway  initiative to support Mercosur’s green and digital transition. As trade volumes grow, Electronic Bills of Lading help by enabling faster, more secure, and fully digital handling of one of the most important documents in international trade.

 

Scenario

A Brazilian Coffee Exporter contracts an Ocean Carrier to ship a container of coffee beans to an European Coffee Importer:

  • Stakeholders: Buyer, Seller and Carrier
  • Origen: Port of Santos, Brazil
  • Destination:  Port of Antwerp, Belgium
  • Goods: Arabica Coffee Beans
  • Amount: 18 tons
  • Container: 1 TEU

 

Booking Request

The exporter requests transportation from the ocean carrier by submitting a booking request.

The booking page of the app call the FREIGHTBOOKING API to create the Ocean Booking on SAP Cloud ERP

Stakeholder Onboarding

Before the shipment process begins, key participants such as the exporter, importer, and carrier are onboarded to the platform. During this step, each party registers its digital identity and public key, enabling secure signing, verification, and ownership transfer of the Electronic Bill of Lading.

Electronic Bill of Lading Lifecycle

The eBL lifecycle is based on the events generated by the Terminal Operating System:

1. Event: Load

Port/Terminal Operating System: confirms the container has been loaded on a vessel

 

Event Payload:

{
  "event_id": "evt-load-0001",
  "event_created_date_time": "2026-03-16T10:15:30Z",

  "event": {
    "journey_type": "EQUIPMENT",
    "event_classifier": "ACT",
    "event_type": "LOAD",
    "transport_mode": "VESSEL",
    "empty_indicator": "FULL"
  },

  "facility": {
    "facility_type": "POTE",
    "location": {
      "location_code": "BRSSZ",
      "location_name": "Port of Santos"
    }
  },

  "transport": {
    "vessel_name": "Atlantic Pearl",
    "voyage_number": "VY-2024-095"
  },

  "equipment": {
    "container_number": "MSKU1234567",
    "iso_equipment_code": "22G1"
  },

  "shipment": {
    "commodity": "Green Coffee Beans",
    "gross_weight_kg": 18000
  },

  "destination": {
    "location_code": "BEANR",
    "location_name": "Port of Antwerp"
  }
}

 

On step 1, the event is published on a topic of SAP Event Mesh

On the step 2, the event is consumed by the Event-to-Business Actions framework that triggers the workflow on SAP Build Process Automation.

On the step 3, an integration flow is triggered on Integration Suite:

 

Integration Suite

On step 4, Integration flow fetches the Ocean Freight Booking on SAP Cloud ERP(Step 5) according to the 'Container ID' and 'Voyage ID' and then proceed with the creation of the Electronic Bill of Lading via TradeTrust Framework.

Business Partners:

Container:

 

Shipping Instructions

After SAP Integration Suite fetch the data from the backend, it generate a payload.

Electronic Bill of Lading Payload:

{
  "$schema": "https://schema.tradetrust.io/ebl/1.0/schema.json",
  "@context": [
    "https://www.w3.org/2018/credentials/v1",
    "https://schema.trustvc.io/credentials/v1"
  ],
  "id": "urn:uuid:sap-ebl-trustvc-001",
  "type": [
    "VerifiableCredential",
    "ElectronicBillOfLading",
    "TradeTrustDocument"
  ],
  "issuers": [
    {
      "name": "Carrier",
      "documentStore": "0x0908aD5481673E19c3D03f1379E0032458118881",
      "identityProof": {
        "type": "DNS-TXT",
        "location": "carrier.example.com"
      }
    }
  ],
  "issuanceDate": "2026-03-16T11:00:00Z",
  "credentialSubject": {
    "id": "did:ethr:0x562eeBbaBB405db740f2e607F87f152989a10b7E",
    "origin": "Port of Santos, Brazil",
    "destination": "Port of Antwerp, Belgium",
    "goods": "Green Coffee Beans",
    "amount": "18 tons",
    "container": "1 TEU",
    "holder": "0x562eeBbaBB405db740f2e607F87f152989a10b7E",
    "consignee": "0x5C1d8d47784d8302B3839436e0e82E094D8e234f"
  }
}

 

On step 6, Integration Suite sends the payload to the TradeTrust framework to issue the eBL(step 7) :

 

Tradetrust framework Node.js deployed on Cloud Foundry.

const fs = require("fs");
const path = require("path");
const { Wallet, ethers } = require("ethers");
const { signDocument, SUPPORTED_SIGNING_ALGORITHM } = require("@tradetrust-tt/tradetrust");
require("dotenv").config();

const RPC_URL = "https://rpc.sepolia.linea.build"; // Linea Sepolia RPC
const PRIVATE_KEY = process.env.PRIVATE_KEY; // Carrier wallet 
const NFT_CONTRACT_ADDRESS = "0x6fD6DB343c5d84Baa7fDaeD5CedDA15652451a8f";

// Stakeholders
const EXPORTER = "0x562eeBbaBB405db740f2e607F87f152989a10b7E";
const IMPORTER = "0x5C1d8d47784d8302B3839436e0e82E094D8e234f";

The payload is wrapped with OpenAttestation metadata and cryptographically signed using the carrier’s key pair (public and private keys).

let rawWrapped = fs.readFileSync(WRAPPED_JSON, "utf-8");
    const wrappedDocument = JSON.parse(rawWrapped);
    console.log("✅ Wrapped document loaded");

    // Sign document with carrier key
    const keyPair = {
      public: "did:ethr:0x9843Cd2834010A4dca776d3035117750d20b3C01#controller",
      private: PRIVATE_KEY
    };

    const signedDocument = await signDocument(
      wrappedDocument,
      SUPPORTED_SIGNING_ALGORITHM.Secp256k1VerificationKey2018,
      keyPair
    );

    fs.writeFileSync(SIGNED_JSON, JSON.stringify(signedDocument, null, 2));
    console.log("Document signed successfully");

Prepare parameters

// Connect wallet & contract
    const provider = new ethers.JsonRpcProvider(RPC_URL);
    const wallet = new Wallet(PRIVATE_KEY, provider);

    console.log("🧾 Wallet (carrier):", wallet.address);
    const balance = await provider.getBalance(wallet.address);
    console.log(" ETH balance:", ethers.formatEther(balance));
    if (balance === 0n) throw new Error("Wallet has ZERO ETH — fund it for gas");

    const contract = new ethers.Contract(NFT_CONTRACT_ADDRESS, EBL_ABI, wallet);

    // Check wallet is carrier
    const carrierAddress = await contract.carrier();
    if (wallet.address.toLowerCase() !== carrierAddress.toLowerCase()) {
      throw new Error(`Wallet is not the carrier. Contract carrier is ${carrierAddress}`);
    }

    //Prepare issueEBL parameters
    const tokenId = BigInt(Date.now()); // PoC uniqueness
    const merkleRoot = signedDocument?.signature?.merkleRoot;
    const documentHash = toBytes32(merkleRoot);

    // Estimate gas + send
    const gasEstimate = await contract.issueEBL.estimateGas(
      tokenId,
      EXPORTER,
      IMPORTER,
      documentHash,
      tokenURI
    );

    const gasLimit = percentBuffer(gasEstimate, 25);

    const maxPriorityFeePerGas = ethers.parseUnits("1", "gwei"); // tip
    const maxFeePerGas = ethers.parseUnits("5", "gwei");         // must be >= priority
    
    const tx = await contract.issueEBL(
      tokenId,
      EXPORTER,
      IMPORTER,
      documentHash,
      tokenURI,
      {
        gasLimit,
        maxFeePerGas,
        maxPriorityFeePerGas
      }
    );
    

    console.log("📤 Transaction sent:", tx.hash);
    const receipt = await tx.wait();
    console.log("✅ Mint/Issue confirmed in block:", receipt.blockNumber);

 

NFT Minted on Blockchain

From this point onward, all TradeTrust-related steps will be demonstrated using the TradeTrust viewer. However, these operations can also be implemented programmatically using the TradeTrust Node.js library.

Electronic Bill of Lading created:

2. Event: Departure

Once the vessel leaves the port and start the journey, the Terminal Operating System emits the event Departure:

{
  "event_id": "evt-depa-0001",
  "event_created_date_time": "2026-03-16T18:40:00Z",
  "journey_event": {
    "journey_type": "TRANSPORT",
    "event_classifier": "ACT",
    "event_type": "DEPA",
    "transport_mode": "VESSEL",
    "facility_type": "POTE"
  },
  "shipment_location": {
    "type_code": "POL",
    "location_code": "BRSSZ"
  },
  "transport": {
    "vessel_name": "Atlantic Pearl",
    "voyage_number": "VY-2024-095"
  },
  "shipment": {
    "transport_document_reference": "BL-SANTOS-ANT-0001"
  }
}

The vessel starts to transmit telemetry(stored on a Hana DB) which is used for tracking and trace:

Once the goods are in transit, the buyer is notified via SAP BPA to provide the payment:

Once the payment is verified, the ownership of the Electronic Bill of Lading can be transferred to the Importer:

3. Event: Arrival

This event notifies the consignee via SAP Process Automation that the goods have arrived at the destination port and it is ready for pick up

Payload:

{
  "event_id": "evt-arri-0001",
  "event_created_date_time": "2026-03-26T07:20:00Z",
  "journey_event": {
    "journey_type": "TRANSPORT",
    "event_classifier": "ACT",
    "event_type": "ARRI",
    "transport_mode": "VESSEL",
    "facility_type": "POTE"
  },
  "shipment_location": {
    "type_code": "POD",
    "location_code": "BEANR"
  },
  "transport": {
    "vessel_name": "Atlantic Pearl",
    "voyage_number": "VY-2024-095"
  },
  "shipment": {
    "transport_document_reference": "BL-SANTOS-ANT-0001"
  }
}

 

Journey completed:

 

The Importer surrenders the electronic Bill of Lading to the carrier for cargo release at the destination port.

The surrender step marks the end of the workflow and completes the trade process.

Endorsement Chain completed:

Conclusion:

This Proof of Concept demonstrates how an electronic Bill of Lading can be issued, verified, and transferred using a blockchain technology integrated with SAP Business Technology Platform.

Given that modern container vessels can carry thousands of containers, the proposed architecture is designed to scale and process a high volume of operational events efficiently.

Looking ahead, AI agents could be integrated to further enhance the solution. These agents could monitor events, detect potential issues, automate operational tasks, and trigger workflows when required.

Combined with digital payment mechanisms such as on-chain settlement, this approach has the potential to significantly transform how international trade processes are executed.

 

Sources: SAP BTP Guidance Framework,  SAP Application Extension Methodology , SAP Integration Solution Advisory Methodology , Integration Architecture Guide.

References:

[1] ICC Academy: Break the eBL silos: Why interoperability is the missing link in digital trade

[2] CargoX: Environmental impact of digitalisation in shipping documentation: Carbon footprint comparison of paper and electronic bills of lading

[3] Weareprocarrier

[4] Intracen: International Trade Centre

[5] IMDA: TradeTrust Legal Analysis Article

[6] Smart Ports Bynder

[7] GitHub - Event to Business Action

[8] Irishweekly: Europe Greenlights Massive Mercosur Trade Deal, Ending Long Stalemate

[9] 93degreescoffeeroasters

[10] ICC Academy: Ebl digital trade

[11] Intracen: Expediting Trade Through Electronic Bills of Lading

entry.title: The Utility Clean Core Blueprint: How SAP BTP Enables Modernization Without Disrupting Regulated OPR
entry.content:
The Utility Clean Core Blueprint: How SAP BTP Enables Modernization Without Disrupting Regulated Operations

 

A White Paper by Atul Joshi

 

Executive Summary

Utilities today face a paradox: they must modernize rapidly to support digital customer expectations, DER integration, and grid intelligence — yet they operate in one of the most regulated, risk‑averse environments in the world. Traditional SAP landscapes, especially SAP IS‑U, are burdened with decades of custom code, point‑to‑point integrations, and rigid processes that make transformation slow, expensive, and operationally risky.

This white paper introduces The Utility Clean Core Blueprint, a modernization model that enables utilities to evolve their SAP landscape without destabilizing missions, critical billing, metering, and customer operations. The blueprint leverages SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) as the innovation and integration layer, allowing utilities to decouple custom logic, orchestrate processes, and adopt cloud capabilities — all while keeping the SAP core stable, compliant, and upgrade‑ready.

 

  1. The Modernization Challenge in Regulated Utilities

 

Utilities operate under unique constraints:

  • Regulatory oversight limits downtime and process changes.
  • High‑volume billing cycles require predictable system performance.
  • Legacy customizations make upgrades risky and expensive.
  • Aging IS‑U systems cannot support modern digital experiences.
  • Integration sprawl slows innovation and increases operational risk.

Most utilities want to move toward S/4HANA Utilities, but the path is blocked by:

  • 10–20 years of Z‑code
  • tightly coupled interfaces
  • custom workflows embedded in IS‑U
  • inflexible monolithic architecture

This is where Clean Core becomes not just a best practice — but a survival strategy.

2. What Clean Core Really Means for Utilities

 

Clean Core is often misunderstood as “removing custom code.” For utilities, it means something deeper:

Clean Core = A stable SAP transactional engine + an agile innovation layer on SAP BTP

This approach allows:

  • predictable billing and metering operations
  • faster innovation cycles
  • safer upgrades
  • cloud‑ready architecture
  • regulatory compliance

In other words: Keep the core clean. Move the complexity out. Modernize without breaking operations.

3. SAP BTP: The Enabler of Utility Modernization

 

SAP BTP provides the capabilities utilities need to modernize safely:

 

Key BTP Services for Utilities

Capability

BTP Service

Utility Value

Event-driven decoupling

Event Mesh

Real-time meter events, outage events, billing triggers

Integration modernization

Integration Suite

Replace PI/PO, eliminate point-to-point interfaces

Extension development

SAP CAP / Kyma

Move Z‑logic out of IS‑U

Workflow automation

BTP Workflow

Field service approvals, credit workflows

Data intelligence

HANA Cloud

Meter analytics, billing insights

Security & governance

IAS/IPS

Unified identity for customer and employee apps

BTP becomes the innovation shell around the SAP core.

 

 

4. The Utility Clean Core Blueprint

 

The Utility Clean Core Blueprint consists of five practical steps:

Step 1 — Stabilize the Core

  • Freeze custom development in IS‑U
  • Identify Z‑objects that block upgrades
  • Classify custom code into: retire, refactor, or externalize
  • Clean up unused interfaces

Step 2 — Externalize Custom Logic to BTP

 

Move the following out of IS‑U:

  • complex billing rules
  • validation logic
  • workflow approvals
  • customer-facing processes
  • meter data transformations

Use CAP, Node.js, or Java on BTP.

Step 3 — Modernize Integrations

Replace:

  • PI/PO mappings
  • RFC-based interfaces
  • batch jobs

With:

  • Event Mesh
  • Integration Suite
  • APIs

Step 4 — Introduce Event-Driven Architecture

Trigger events for:

  • meter reads
  • billing completion
  • move-in/move-out
  • credit actions
  • outage notifications

This decouples processes and reduces core load.

Step 5 — Enable Coexistence with S/4HANA

Run IS‑U + S/4HANA + BTP in hybrid mode:

  • IS‑U remains the billing engine
  • S/4 handles finance, procurement, asset management
  • BTP orchestrates innovation

This avoids a risky “big bang” migration.

5. Practical Utility Case Studies (Realistic, Practitioner-Level)

Case Study 1: Reducing Billing Run Failures with BTP Event Mesh

A North American utility struggled with billing run failures caused by custom validations embedded in IS‑U.

Problem: Every enhancement point slowed down billing and caused unpredictable dumps. Solution:

  • Move validation logic to a CAP service on BTP
  • Trigger validation via Event Mesh before billing
  • Return results via API

Outcome:

  • Billing runtime reduced by 27%
  • Zero dumps in 3 months
  • Clean Core achieved without touching core billing logic

Case Study 2: Modernizing Move-In/Move-Out Without Touching IS‑U

 

A regulated utility needed a digital customer onboarding experience.

Problem: IS‑U move-in/out processes were too rigid and heavily customized.

Solution:

  • Build a customer onboarding app on BTP
  • Orchestrate workflow using BTP Workflow
  • Trigger IS‑U move-in via API only at the final step

Outcome:

  • Customer onboarding time reduced from 3 days to 30 minutes
  • No changes required in IS‑U core
  • Regulatory compliance maintained

 

Case Study 3: Eliminating 40+ Point-to-Point Integrations

Problem: European utility had PI/PO interfaces that were expensive to maintain.

Solution:

  • Replace PI/PO with Integration Suite
  • Introduce canonical data models
  • Use Event Mesh for asynchronous processes

Outcome:

  • Integration failures reduced by 60%
  • Upgrade downtime reduced by 40%
  • IT operations cost reduced significantly

 

6. The Coexistence Architecture: IS‑U + S/4 + BTP

Utilities cannot afford a big-bang migration. The coexistence model allows:

IS‑U

→ Billing, metering, device management

S/4HANA

→ Finance, procurement, asset management

SAP BTP

→ Innovation, integration, automation, analytics

This architecture supports:

  • gradual modernization
  • regulatory stability
  • continuous innovation

7. Governance: The Missing Piece in Most Utility Programs

Clean Core is not a technical exercise — it is a governance discipline.

Key governance principles

  • No custom code in the core
  • All new extensions must be BTP-first
  • Event-driven architecture preferred over batch
  • API-led integration
  • Quarterly review of Z‑objects
  • Architecture board approval for all enhancements

This is how utilities stay modern after the transformation.

8. Conclusion: Modernize Without Disruption

Utilities cannot pause operations to modernize. They need a blueprint that:

  • protects regulated processes
  • reduces technical debt
  • accelerates innovation
  • prepares for S/4HANA
  • supports the energy transition

 

The Utility Clean Core Blueprint provides exactly that.

 

By using SAP BTP as the innovation layer, utilities can modernize safely, incrementally, and intelligently — without destabilizing the mission-critical systems that keep the lights on.

entry.title: Custom Joule Agent for SAP Ariba SLP - Building an AI Agent for Supplier Registration Approval
entry.content:
The Premise

I'll be honest — I've built this exact workflow before.

In previous posts, I put together a supplier registration approval automation using SAP Build Process Automation. It worked, but it took me a significant amount of time to complete: process flows, variables, connections, data mappings, error handling — each piece designed and wired manually. You can check out those articles Extending SLP functionalities with SAP BTP and Artificial Intelligence  and SAP Ariba SLP - Optical character recognition and Extraction for Supplier Registration Process .

This time, I completed a fully functional proof of concept in less than 7 days.

Same business problem. Same outcome. A fraction of the effort.

Going in, I had all the questions a developer instinctively asks: Who manages the data and how? Where do variables live? How are connections handled? What happens when something fails? These are the questions that typically consume the first week of any integration project before a single line of logic is written.

With Joule Studio, most of those questions answered themselves. The agent managed context between steps, retained the values I needed across the workflow, and handled the SAP Ariba skill connections natively — often without me explicitly instructing it to. The infrastructure just disappeared.

What remained was the part that actually matters: defining the business logic, the scoring model, the decision rules, and the communication to the supplier. The work that requires domain knowledge, not plumbing.

The Real Cost of Manual Supplier Review

Every supplier onboarding in SAP Ariba SLP follows the same predictable path. The supplier fills out a registration questionnaire. An approval task lands in someone's queue. That someone opens it, reads through the answers, checks the numbers, looks for the documents, and makes a call: approve, send it back, or reject.

Sounds simple. It really doesn't feel that way at month-end when the queue has 40 tasks in it.

Here's what always got me: most of what a reviewer is doing in that queue isn't actually a judgment call. They're reading a free-text answer asking themselves — does this BCP response mention an RPO? Does this AML section describe a whistleblowing channel? They're checking if a growth rate in one field matches a balance change in another. They're confirming a document is attached. That's not analysis. That's a checklist. A very slow, very manual, very inconsistent checklist being run by people whose time is worth a lot more than that.

And the problems that came from it were real:

  • Reviews varied wildly depending on who did them — the same questionnaire could sail through with one reviewer and generate three rounds of back-and-forth with another, often over a misspelled word or an answer that was technically correct but phrased oddly.
  • Tasks just sat there — not because nobody cared, but because reviewers had other priorities and supplier registration wasn't always the urgent thing. Suppliers would wait days on submissions that should have been a five-minute review.
  • Nothing was documented — approvals went through with no written rationale. The moment someone asked "why was this supplier approved?" the answer was essentially "because someone clicked approve."
  • Language became a routing problem — suppliers in Argentina or Brazil submitting in Spanish or Portuguese meant we couldn't just assign the task to anyone. It either waited for the right person or got reviewed by someone who couldn't properly read the answers.

None of these are hard problems. They're consistency problems. And the fix isn't hiring more reviewers — it's stopping the pattern-matching work from reaching a human in the first place.

That's where this started.

How it works?

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

How it looks?

 

How can I build my own?

Before anything else, you'll need to set up Joule Studio inside SAP Build. If you haven't done that yet, this guide walks you through it - How to get Started with Joule Studio 

Once you're in, you'll notice two new options in the Build lobby: Create a Joule Agent and Create a Joule Skill. Think of skills as the hands of the agent — they are the individual actions it can take. The agent is the brain that decides when and how to use them.

For this project, we built 4 custom skills, each one mapped to a specific SAP Ariba API call:

SkillWhat It Does
getPendingTasksRetrieves all active approval tasks from SAP Ariba SLP
getTaskDetailsFetches full task data including document ID, URL, and approval chain
getQuestionsAnswersPulls the supplier's questionnaire responses by document ID
postDecisionPosts the approval decision and comment back to the task in Ariba

These four skills are the only connection points between the agent and SAP Ariba. Everything else — the analysis, the scoring, the decision logic, the multilingual handling — lives entirely inside the agent configuration. No additional middleware, no custom code.

In Joule Studio, the foundation of every skill is the destination and the action endpoint. This is fundamentally different from how SAP Build Process Automation handles connectivity. In BPA, you could get away with loosely configured connections and patch things as you went. In Joule Studio, that doesn't work. Each skill needs a correctly defined destination in SAP BTP — the right authentication, the right base URL, the right headers — and each action within that skill needs a precisely mapped endpoint. Get either of those wrong and the skill fails silently, or worse, returns data you can't trust.

Check the SAP BTP Destinations section, it will give you the base for setting Destinations - End to End guide to setup Joule, SAP Build Process Automation, and SAP Build Work Zone on SAP BTP 

Let's make this concrete. Below is the actual configuration of getPendingTasks — the first skill the agent calls every time it runs. On the left you can see the destination binding pointing to the SAP Ariba environment. Below it, the action defines the specific endpoint the skill hits, the HTTP method, and the parameters it passes. This is the complete bridge between Joule Studio and Ariba. No code, no transformation layer — just a correctly configured destination and a precisely defined action

getPendingTasks Joule Skill

Pending Task Destination Response

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

What about the AI Agent?

Once your skills are ready, you create the agent. Joule Studio gives you three sections to configure. Each one has a distinct job — and understanding the boundary between them is what makes the difference between an agent that works reliably and one that kind of works most of the time.

 

Expertise — Who the agent is

This is where you define the agent's identity, role, and domain knowledge. Not what it does step by step, but what it knows going in. Think of it as the briefing you'd give a new analyst before their first day on the job.

In our case, we defined the agent as Joule, embedded in SAP Ariba SLP, with deep knowledge of supplier onboarding workflows, approval mechanics, questionnaire interpretation, and the SLP object model. We spelled out the domain concepts — tasks, workspaces, approval requests, registration projects — so the agent could interpret ambiguous API data through the right lens. When a financial number looked inconsistent, it needed procurement context to judge it correctly. This section provided that foundation.

Instructions — What the agent does

This is the behavioral core. A sequential set of rules that tells the agent exactly how to act, in what order, and under what conditions. Every step, every trigger, every output format lives here.

In our case, this section contained the full seven-step workflow: filtering tasks, building the results table, triggering the questionnaire analysis on a specific user command, running the three-part scoring analysis automatically, and posting the decision without waiting for confirmation. We also defined the comment templates for each decision type and the language rule for multilingual suppliers here. If the Expertise section is the briefing, Instructions is the playbook.

Additional Context — The rules the agent looks up

This is the reference layer. Static mappings, lookup tables, scoring rubrics, decision logic, and field definitions that the agent consults during execution. It doesn't drive behavior directly — Instructions does that — but it gives the agent the structured data it needs to make the right calls.

In our case, this section held everything that would be too verbose to embed in Instructions: the full decision mapping (Approve / Deny / DenyWithResubmit with priority rules), the answer lookup table mapping qualifiedNumber fields to their meaning, the complete scoring checklists for all four questions, the financial flag thresholds, the compliance flag definitions, and the language rule for comment output. Keeping this separate from Instructions made both sections easier to read, easier to update, and easier to debug when something didn't behave as expected.

TIP💡 A rule of thumb that saved us a lot of iteration: if it describes behavior, it belongs in Instructions. If it describes data or rules the behavior depends on, it belongs in Additional Context. Mixing the two is the most common reason agent configurations become hard to maintain.

Final Thoughts

I want to be honest about something before wrapping this up.

This agent doesn't replace human judgment. It was never meant to. There will always be submissions that land in a grey area — answers that technically check the boxes but still feel incomplete, numbers that pass the threshold but don't quite add up, situations that no rulebook covers cleanly. Those still need a person to look at them, for that we can add a "Human node" on Ariba SLP to make sure a human reviews AI decision.

What the agent does is make sure those are the only cases that reach a human. Everything else — the clean submissions, the straightforward approvals, the obvious denials — gets handled automatically, documented properly, and closed. In most onboarding queues, that's the majority of the work.

What genuinely surprised me wasn't the time I saved, though going from a much longer build down to seven days is hard to dismiss. It was how differently I had to think about the problem.

A few things I'd tell you to pay attention to before you start:

  • Do not rush the destinations. I know it looks like a simple setup step. It isn't. Get the BTP destination and the endpoint actions right before you touch anything else. Every problem I had early on traced back to this. HERE IS WHERE YOU DATA TYPE WILL BE DEFINED.
  • Put the real work into Additional Context. The scoring rubrics, the decision mapping, the language rules — that's where the agent gets smart. A generic configuration gives you a generic agent. The domain-specific rules are what make it actually useful.
  • Test with messy data, not just happy paths. Run a clean approval, a DenyWithResubmit with missing elements, a hard Deny, and at least one Spanish or Portuguese submission. If those four scenarios work correctly, you're in good shape.
  • Always filter on title, not description. The description field can come back empty from the API. I learned this the hard way so you don't have to.

Last but no least let me share some value insights of this PoC

entry.title: Transforming Manual Sales Order Processing into an Intelligent SAP BTP Workflow
entry.content:
Many organizations still rely on manual, email-driven sales order processing. Business users receive customer documents, extract key details, validate information, and create orders in SAP S/4HANA. While manageable at low volumes, this approach becomes time-consuming, error-prone, and difficult to scale as document complexity and volume increase.

Reimagining the Process with SAP BTP

This solution transforms the traditional process into an intelligent, end-to-end workflow using:

  • SAP Build Process Automation for orchestration
  • SAP Document AI for document understanding
  • SAP CAP (Cloud Application Programming Model) for validation, enrichment, and user interaction

The result is a centralized, application-driven experience that minimizes manual effort while maintaining business control.

Solution Overview

The automated workflow replaces fragmented manual steps with a streamlined process:

  1. Incoming emails are monitored via SAP Build Process Automation
  2. Attachments are sent to SAP Document AI for data extraction
  3. Document AI extracts relevant sales order fields using a custom schema
  4. A CAP application retrieves and processes the extracted data
  5. Additional validation and enrichment is performed (e.g., master data checks, missing fields, amount validation)
  6. Business users review and approve data in a centralized interface before posting to SAP S/4HANA

Architecture Highlights

The solution is built on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP):

  • SAP Build Process Automation → event-driven orchestration
  • SAP Document AI → document extraction
  • SAP CAP → business logic, validation, enrichment, UI
  • SAP S/4HANA → system of record
  • Connectivity & Destinations → secure integration via APIs

This architecture enables scalable automation with human-in-the-loop control.

Why This Approach Matters

Document extraction alone is not enough. True automation requires business context, validation, and enrichment.

The CAP layer bridges this gap by:

  • Combining extracted data with SAP master data
  • Applying business rules and validations
  • Providing a controlled user review interface

This ensures accuracy while reducing manual effort.

Key Advantages

  • Reduced manual handoffs
  • Improved validation and enrichment control
  • Enhanced user experience through a centralized interface
  • Seamless integration with SAP S/4HANA
  • Scalable and future-ready architecture

Business Impact

This solution addresses the core challenge of converting unstructured documents into structured SAP transactions.

Customer results include:

  • ~30% increase in order-processing capacity
  • ~90% reduction in manual effort per transaction
  • Improved accuracy and reduced rework
  • Faster month-end and quarter-end close
  • Cleaner audit trail and compliance

Conclusion

By consolidating extraction, validation, enrichment, and user review into a single workflow, organizations can move from manual processing to intelligent automation - improving efficiency while maintaining control.

If you have any further questions, please leave a comment below or send your inquiries to the email CoE_AI_PlanningSpendExec@global.corp.sap. We will be happy to get back to you.

entry.title: Triggering SAP CPI iflow through SAP Build Process Automation

 5. Create ‘Project’ ztestingprj_iflow under Connectors->Actions as below-

a.  Create ‘Action’ zjoule_action under project ‘ztestingprj_iflow’ with URL [/http/ztest_joule] from endpoint generated from CPI iflow ‘test_iflow’.

 20. In ‘Triggers’ tab, create a trigger as per required schedule-

entry.title: Working with DMS in SAP Build Process Automation — A Complete API Guide
entry.content:
Introduction: In one of my recent projects, I had a scenario where I needed to handle Excel files to create or modify mass cost centers. For this, I definitely needed a way to manage files — so I used DMS (Document Management Service)to handle the file operations. 

DMS (Document Management Service) is a key service in SAP BTP for managing files in the cloud. When working with SAP Build Process Automation (BPA), DMS serves as the file storage — when a user uploads a file through a Form Trigger, it is stored in DMS.

Note: Upload attachments to forms, you need an active SAP Document Management Service subscription. With an active subscription, you must then configure your attachment destinations. Before an attachment destination is configured, the attachment option isn't visible when editing a form (Configure SAP Document Management Service for Process Attachments | SAP Help Portal).

More importantly, you need to know how to download files from DMS. Consider a scenario where you need to validate an uploaded file — say, perform Excel-based validations while the file is on the local desktop. After all processing is complete, you can delete the file from DMS. This is the right approach for maintaining storage hygiene in certain scenarios (not all).

You should also understand how to upload files to DMS. Before moving into BPA development, it is essential to test the DMS APIs independently using Bruno (or any other API testing tool like Postman). This blog walks you through exactly that.

Prerequisites

  1. SAP BTP Subaccount with DMS entitlement enabled
  2. DMS Service Instance & Service Key created
  3. Bruno (or any API testing tool) installed
  4. DMS Destination configured in SAP BTP
    • To create a destination from scratch, refer to the official documentation:
      Create Destinations from Scratch – SAP Help
    • After creating the destination, it will appear under Control Tower → Destinations
    • Use a testing tool to verify the connection is working before integrating with BPA

Note: Before connecting BPA directly to the destination, always validate the APIs independently using Bruno.

Step 1: Authentication – Get Access Token 

To perform any CRUD operation on DMS, you need a Bearer Token for authentication. Here's how to obtain it in Bruno:

  1. Open the Auth tab
  2. Select OAuth 2.0 from the dropdown
  3. Set Grant Type to Client Credentials
  4. Fill in the following fields:

Name Value

Access Token URL

<token-url>/oauth/token

Client ID

<your-client-id>

Client Secret

<your-client-secret>

  1. Click Get Access Token — the token will appear under the Grant Type section

Additional Setup:

  • API Key: If required, add it under the Headers tab
  • Proxy: You must configure a proxy for authentication; otherwise, the request will fail

Step 2: Download a Document

To download a document from DMS, use the following:

Method: GET
URL: <base-url>/browser/<repository-id>/root

Query Parameters:

Name Value

objectId

<file-or-folder-id>

cmisselector

properties

filter

cmis:name

BPA Script – Get File Properties:

let options = {
    method:'GET',
    resolveBodyOnly:true,
    responseType:'json',
    timeout:59000
}
if(fileId){
options.url="/root?objectId=" + fileId.split('folderid:')[1];
}
return options;

 

 

BPA Script – Retrieve File Name:

//retriving file name 
let filename =folderId.objects[0].object.properties["cmis:name"].value;
return filename;

BPA Script – Generate Download URL and Headers:

//Generating the URL and Headers Payload
return{
    url:destination.destinationConfiguration.url+ '/root?objectId='+fileId+'&cmisSelector=content',
    options:{
        headers:{
            Authorization: 'Bearer ' + destination.authTokens[0].value
        }
    }
};

 

Step 3: Get Folder Details 

Method: GET
URL: <base-url>/browser/<repository-id>/root

Query Parameters:

Type Name Value

Param

objectId

<folder-object-id>

Param

cmisselector

properties

Param

filter

cmis:objectTypeId

Param

filter

cmis:name

Auth: Pass your access token under Auth tab → Bearer Token

Note: you have to give proxy to for authentication otherwise it will not work 

BPA Script - To get folder details

let options = {
    method:'GET',
    url:'/root?objectId='+objectId.split('folderid:')[1]+'&cmisselector=properties',
    responseType:'json',
    timeout:59000
}
return options;

Step 4: Upload a Document

Use this when you need to upload a file to DMS via BPA — for example, when receiving a file from a different source.

Method: POST
URL: <base-url>/browser/<repository-id>/root

Body (Multipart Form):

Key Value

file

<select your file>

propertyId[0]

cmis:objectTypeId

propertyValue[0]

cmis:document

propertyId[1]

cmis:name

propertyValue[1]

<file-name>

Query Parameters:

Name Value

cmisaction

createDocument

BPA Script - to Upload File

 var options = {
        method: 'POST',
        url: '/root?cmisaction=createDocument', 
        responseType: 'json',
        resolveBodyOnly: true,
        metadataType: irpa_core.enums.request.metadataType.formData,
        metadata: [
            { name: 'propertyId[0]', value: 'cmis:name' },
            { name: 'propertyValue[0]', value: fileName },
            { name: 'propertyId[1]', value: 'cmis:objectTypeId' },
            { name: 'propertyValue[1]', value: 'cmis:document' },
            { name: 'media', file: filePath }
        ]
};
return options;

Step 5: Create Folder

To create a folder within DM,  use the following

Method: POST
URL: <base-url>/browser/<repository-id>/root

Body (Form Data):

Name Value

cmisaction

createFolder

propertyId[0]

cmis:objectTypeId

propertyValue[0]

cmis:folder

propertyId[1]

cmis:name

propertyValue[1]

<folder-name>

BPA Script

 var options = {
        method: 'POST',
        url: '/root?',
        responseType: 'json',
        resolveBodyOnly: true,
        metadataType: irpa_core.enums.request.metadataType.formData,
        metadata: [
            {name:'cmisaction', value: 'createFolder'},
            { name: 'propertyId[0]', value: 'cmis:objectTypeId' },
            { name: 'propertyValue[0]', value: 'cmis:folder' },
            { name: 'propertyId[1]', value: 'cmis:name' },
            { name: 'propertyValue[1]', value: folderName },
        ]
};
return options;

Step 6: Delete a Document/Folder

To delete a document or folder tree from DMS, use the following:

Method: POST
URL: <base-url>/browser/<repository-id>/root

Body (Form Data):

Name Value

cmisaction

deleteTree

objectId

<file-or-folder-id>

unfileObjects

delete

continueOnFailure

true

 

BPA Script - To delete

let objectId = folderId.split('folderid:')[1];
let options = {
    method: 'POST',
    resolveBodyOnly: true,
    responseType: 'json',
    metadataType: irpa_core.enums.request.metadataType.formData,
    form:{
        cmisaction:'deleteTree',
        objectId: objectId,
        unfileObjects:'delete',
        continueOnFailure:true
    }
};
return options;

 

Conclusion :

And that's a wrap! By now, you should have a solid understanding of how to work with DMS in the context of SAP Build Process Automation. We covered the full lifecycle — downloading files to your local machine for validation, getting folder details to inspect what's stored, uploading documents from external sources, creating folders to keep things organized, and deleting files once they've served their purpose.

The key takeaway? Always test your DMS APIs  before wiring them into BPA. It's a small step that saves hours of debugging later. Once you're confident the APIs work as expected, integrating them into your automation becomes straightforward.

I hope this guide helps you build more robust and well-tested BPA workflows. If you have questions or run into issues, feel free to drop a comment — happy to help!.

entry.title: SAP Build Process Automation Workflow from a Custom SAP Fiori Application - My experiences
entry.content:
This blog is to share my experience with integrating SAP UI5 task into SBPA(SAP Build Process Automation ) best way to start integration of SAP UI5 task into Build Process Automation .

https://developers.sap.com/tutorials/spa-create-sapui5-task-orderapproval.html

SAP uses predefined templating to create these workflow task as per above learning  journey . 

There is blog post which says one use custom fiori application to trigger . i tried this approach  first . my observations are mostly around this . 

https://community.sap.com/t5/application-development-and-automation-discussions/triggering-sap-build-process-automation-workflow-from-a-custom-sap-fiori/m-p/13941417#:~:text=Click%20on%20configure%20button%20and%20add%20input.&text=Now%20our%20api%20trigger%20is,be%20created%20in%20BTP%20cockpit.&text=Ensure%20the%20destination%20is%20correctly,inputs%20required%20for%20your%20workflow .

This allows me to trigger Build Process Workflow but this configuration doesn't allow me to consider current user context also these custom fiori apps cant used in Build Process Automation as UI5 Tasks  . 

Observed a difference how URL is generated when these applications  are deployed to HTML5 repository . Apps which uses workflow templates are MTA Based & Custom fiori application used Manage App Router  , because of these custom fiori apps cant integrated as UI5 tasks in SBPA.  

URL for Workflow based 

URL for Custom Fiori App 

 .

even with Custom fiori application i am not able create workflow when i debug i found that SBPA needs a IRPA api key while making call to API . 

how  to get API key is explained  below .  

1) Navigate to Build Process Control Tower . 

2) Go to Environment where are workflows Deployed '

3). API Keys and add API Key , select the scope and create the key at the end of the you will have KEY which can be used in the UI5 application .

entry.title: Part 3 - Workflow n8n Integration with Joule Studio
entry.content:
SAP Community — Technology Blogs n8n meets SAP — 3-Part Series
Part 3 of 3

Workflow n8n Integration with Joule Studio

Series Overview

  1. Enterprise Automation — Deploying n8n on SAP BTP Kyma
  2. Create a Custom Language Model Node for SAP AI Core
  3. Workflow n8n Integration with Joule Studio 📍

In the first two parts of this series we deployed n8n on SAP BTP, Kyma runtime and built a custom node that connects n8n's AI Agent to SAP AI Core's Generative AI Hub. In Part 2 we also built a concrete workflow, the IT Helpdesk Assistant, an agent that can query tickets, knowledge articles, and employee skills from a live OData API and answer questions in natural language.

In this final part, we take that workflow one step further. Instead of accessing it only through n8n's own chat interface, we connect it to Joule Studio so that a Joule agent can discover and call the n8n workflow tools directly. The bridge between the two systems is the Model Context Protocol (MCP), an open standard for exposing tools to AI agents in a structured, discoverable way.

1 Publish the Workflow in n8n

Before anything else, the IT Helpdesk Agent workflow from Part 2 needs to be active and running on your Kyma n8n instance. Open your live n8n instance at your Kyma domain URL, navigate to the workflow and click the Publish Button in the top-right corner of the editor.

An published workflow means its trigger is live and listening. Without the workflow being published, it will not appear in the MCP Server's tool list.

 

 

2 Enable the MCP Server in n8n

n8n has a built-in MCP Server feature that exposes all active workflows with a Chat Trigger as discoverable tools — following the Model Context Protocol standard. Any MCP-compatible client, including Joule Studio, can connect to this endpoint and see exactly which tools are available and how to call them.

To enable it, go to Settings in your n8n instance and navigate to the MCP Server section. Enable the feature. n8n will generate two pieces of information that you will need in the next steps:

MCP Server URLThe base URL of your n8n instance — the same Kyma domain URL from Part 1, for example https://n8n.<your-kyma-domain>
Access TokenA bearer token generated by n8n that MCP clients must present with every request to authenticate themselves

Copy both values and keep them at hand — you will use them when creating the BTP Destination in the next step.

 

Treat the MCP access token like any other credential. Anyone who holds this token can call your n8n workflows remotely. Do not share it, do not commit it to a repository, and rotate it if you suspect it has been exposed.
 
 
The n8n Settings page with the MCP Server section open — showing the feature enabled and the generated Access Token and URL.

 

 

3 Create the BTP Destination

Joule Studio does not call external services directly. It goes through the SAP BTP Destination Service — a managed proxy layer that handles connectivity configurations centrally. To connect Joule to n8n, we register the n8n MCP endpoint as a destination in our BTP subaccount and add a set of properties that tell Joule Studio this destination is an MCP server.

Open the Destination Configuration

In the SAP BTP Cockpit, navigate to your subaccount and open Connectivity → Destinations. Create New Destination.

Basic Configuration

Fill in the destination properties as follows:

NameA descriptive name you will recognise in Joule Studio, for example n8n-helpdesk-mcp
TypeHTTP
URLThe base URL of your n8n Kyma instance — for example https://n8n.<your-kyma-domain>
AuthenticationNo Authentication — the token is passed separately via an additional property below

Additional Properties

Below the basic fields, there is an Additional Properties section. This is where the MCP-specific configuration lives. Add the following two entries:

Key Value
sap-joule-studio-mcp-servertrue
URL.headers.AuthorizationThe access token from n8n — paste the full token value here

The first property flags this destination to Joule Studio as an MCP server — without it, the destination will not appear in the MCP server selector inside Joule Studio. The second property injects the n8n access token as an Authorization header on every outbound request, which is how n8n authenticates incoming MCP calls.

Click Save.

 


The completed BTP Destination form — showing the Name, URL, Authentication  and both Additional Properties

 

4 Create the Agent in Joule Studio

With the destination in place, open Joule Studio from your BTP Cockpit subscriptions. Navigate to the Agents section and create a new agent.

Agent Identity

Give the agent a clear name and fill in the description fields:

NameFor example IT Helpdesk Assistant
DescriptionA short explanation of what the agent does — this is displayed to end users. For example: "Assists with IT support requests by looking up tickets, knowledge articles, and employee skills."
ExpertiseRole or Persona for the Agent eg. "IT Helpdesk assistant"
InstructionsA more detailed prompt that guides the agent's reasoning. Describe the domain it operates in and any behavioral expectations.

5 Connect the MCP Server

Below the model settings section, Joule Studio provides an option to add an MCP Server to the agent. This is where you link the BTP Destination you created in the previous step.

 

1. Select the MCP Server

Click Add MCP Server. A selector will appear listing all BTP Destinations in your subaccount that have the sap-joule-studio-mcp-server: true property set. Select the destination you created — for example n8n-helpdesk-mcp.

 

2. Set the Path in Advanced Options

Expand the Advanced Options section for the MCP server entry. Set the Path field to:

/mcp-server/http/

This path is the MCP endpoint that n8n exposes on its server. It tells Joule Studio exactly where within the destination URL to send MCP discovery and tool call requests.

 

Joule Studio — the MCP Server section with the destination selected and the Advanced Options expanded

 

3. Verify the Tool List

After saving the MCP server configuration, Joule Studio will attempt to connect to the n8n MCP endpoint and retrieve the list of available tools. If the destination, token, and path are all correct, a list will appear directly in the interface showing every active n8n tools.

 

Seeing the tool list appear is the confirmation that the full integration is working end-to-end. Joule Studio has successfully discovered the n8n MCP server, authenticated using the token from the destination, and retrieved the tool schema. No further wiring is needed — the agent can now call these tools in a live conversation.
 
Click Apply.

Joule Studio — the tool list populated after a successful MCP connection

 

 

What to Check if the Tool List Does Not Appear

If the list remains empty or an error is shown, work through the following in order:

  • Confirm them MCP Server is enabled in n8n
  • Check that the URL in the BTP Destination points to the correct Kyma domain and does not have a trailing slash conflict with the path.
  • Verify the access token in URL.headers.Authorization is current — n8n may rotate or invalidate tokens when the MCP Server is reconfigured.
  • Ensure the path in Joule Studio's Advanced Options is exactly /mcp-server/http/ including the trailing slash.

6 Test the Agent

Save the agent configuration and open the built-in test inside Joule Studio. Type a question that maps to one of the tool's capabilities — for example:  "What are the currently open tickets?" 

Joule will process the message, identify the appropriate tool, send an MCP tool call through the BTP Destination to n8n, and receive the OData response. The final answer presented in the chat will be a summary of the live data now surfaced through Joule's conversational interface.

 
Joule Studio test chat with the Agent reasoning

 

 

 

✓ Series Complete

Over three posts we built a complete, production-oriented integration stack connecting n8n and the SAP ecosystem — from infrastructure to AI to conversational interfaces.


Part 1 Deployed n8n self-hosted on SAP BTP Kyma runtime with persistent storage and an external API Rule
Part 2 Built a custom n8n node bridging SAP AI Core's Generative AI Hub and deployed an IT Helpdesk Agent workflow
Part 3 Exposed the n8n workflow as an MCP server, connected it to Joule Studio via a BTP Destination and gave a Joule agent access 
 
 
 

AI Usage Disclosure: Gen AI was used exclusively for linguistic refinements such as grammar, spelling, and phrasing as well as for the structural organisation of this text. All conceptual content, technical knowledge, architecture decisions, and implementation steps were developed independently by the author.

 
SAP Community — Technology Blogs n8n meets SAP — Part 3 of 3

entry.title: Why SAP Document AI Matters More Than Most Enterprises Think
entry.content:
Introduction

When everyone gets excited about the same topics, conversations will naturally center around them. Eventually, that excitement will catch on, and you’ll find yourself on the same wavelength as everyone else. Currently, as businesses focus on enterprise AI projects, most discussions revolve around similar themes: copilot, agents, and large language models. However, despite significant investments in AI, many businesses still face a common operational obstacle: documents.

Regardless of the industry, invoices, purchase orders, supplier forms, shipping documents, contracts, and compliance documents flow between departments daily. Each requires some degree of manual verification or validation and often involves multiple systems or workflows. This is where SAP Document AI comes in.

SAP Document AI transforms unstructured business documents into structured operational data, helping businesses modernize their operations. Instead of just digitizing your documents, you can use Document AI to make them useful and functional within your business. Even with advancements in intelligent front-end experiences, the foundational infrastructure supporting these solutions still relies on manual verification, various data extraction methods, and disconnected workflows. Document intelligence within SAP environments has matured, and companies now view it as a key automation initiative rather than a secondary option.

Our Experience

Adopting Document AI can reduce the need for labor to support business functions, leading to more efficient solutions. SAP Document AI addresses a common challenge in enterprise environments: extracting, understanding, and organizing information from business documents. In many companies, important operational data arrives in formats like PDFs, scanned files, emails, invoices, and forms, which ERP systems struggle to process without manual help.

SAP Document AI lowers this friction by using AI to identify document types, extract relevant fields, classify information, and prepare that data for business workflows. This means organizations can automate many document-heavy processes across finance, procurement, supply chain, HR, and other operational areas, rather than having employees manually review documents and transfer information between systems. The value of using Document AI goes beyond faster document processing; it empowers operational workflows to operate with greater speed, consistency, and scalability. As enterprises manage more operational data, minimizing manual document reliance becomes essential for maintaining efficiency.

We had the opportunity to see this first-hand. In this scenario, the company was operating in the oil and gas industry, where customer representatives and field engineers constantly generate operational documents while working across remote locations. Every service ticket, carrier document, export filing, client approval, invoice, or credit memo needed to be attached to the correct Sales Order inside SAP for compliance and operational traceability purposes. The challenge was that these documents arrived through emails in different languages, layouts, and formats, often with little context beyond a subject line. As a result, employees had to manually open every file, identify what type of document it was, locate the Sales Order reference somewhere inside the content, rename the file according to internal naming conventions, and upload it into SAP. At a scale of more than 8,000 documents per month, this translated into over 1,000 hours of manual effort every month, distributed across teams in multiple regions.

The organization already had partial automation in place through legacy capture tools and batch jobs, but the process still depended heavily on human intervention before the automation could even begin. A single typo in a file name or an incorrectly classified document could silently break the workflow and prevent the document from being linked correctly inside SAP. By implementing SAP Document AI on SAP BTP, the process shifted from manual interpretation to intelligent document processing. Incoming documents were automatically picked up through SAP Integration Suite, classified using SAP Document AI, and processed to extract key operational data such as Sales Order numbers, vendor details, amounts, and document dates. The extracted information was then validated and routed directly into SAP workflows, significantly reducing manual effort while improving scalability, consistency, and operational visibility across the entire process.

Why This Matters Beyond Document Processing

What made this project valuable was not just the automation itself. I would say it was rather the impact it had on daily operations. Once documents could be processed and validated in a more consistent way, teams spent far less time trying to interpret files manually or fixing small mistakes that slowed everything down. Workflows became easier to manage across different regions, and the process no longer depended so heavily on people knowing specific naming conventions or internal steps that were never formally documented.

Over time, we also realized that SAP Document AI was doing much more than helping with document processing. It became part of the operational structure supporting the entire workflow. As companies continue investing in SAP Business AI and intelligent workflows, having reliable operational data becomes increasingly important. If documents are still disconnected from the systems around them, scaling automation becomes much harder than expected.

Conclusion

As we said at the beginning, a lot of the conversation around enterprise AI focuses on what people can immediately see: copilots, AI agents, chat interfaces, and new user experiences. At the same time, many operational problems still happen behind the scenes, especially in processes where important business information is buried inside emails, PDFs, scanned files, and manual workflows.

Our experience with SAP Document AI showed us that document intelligence is no longer just a secondary automation tool. It can play an important role in helping businesses create cleaner and more scalable operations. When documents become structured operational data instead of isolated files, workflows move faster, teams spend less time on repetitive tasks, and enterprise systems become easier to scale over time.

entry.title: Principal Propagation from SAP Build Process Automation workflow to S/4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Introduction

When building automated workflows in SAP Build Process Automation, a common requirement is to call backend APIs in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud on behalf of the user who triggered the workflow, not a generic technical user. This is where principal propagation becomes essential.
Principal propagation ensures that the identity of the originating user is securely forwarded through the call chain, allowing S/4HANA to enforce its own authorization checks based on who actually initiated the action. Without it, all requests reach the backend under a shared service account, which undermines auditability, segregation of duties, and fine-grained access control.
In this article, I walk through the exact configuration steps I followed to enable principal propagation between SAP Build Process Automation and SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, covering the trust setup on SAP BTP, the destination configuration, and the corresponding communication arrangement on the S/4HANA side. If you are facing the same challenge, this guide should give you a clear, step-by-step path to get it working.

Prerequisites

Before you begin, make sure you have:
  • An SAP BTP subaccount with SAP Build Process Automation enabled
  • Access to your SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud system with an administrator role
  • An existing workflow in SAP Build Process Automation that needs to call an S/4HANA API

Step 1 — Configure S/4HANA Public Cloud

1.1 Create a Communication User

In the SAP S/4HANA Fiori launchpad, navigate to Communication Management > Maintain Communication Users and create a dedicated user for inbound communication. Make sure you save the password; this will be needed in configuring the Destination.

1.2 Create a Communication Scenario

Navigate to Communication Management > Communication Scenarios and either select an existing scenario that exposes the API you need or create a custom one. Note the scenario ID — you will need it in the next step.
Regardless of whether you use an existing or custom scenario, make sure that OAuth 2.0 is enabled as a supported authorization method under the inbound communication settings of the scenario. This is a prerequisite for the SAML bearer assertion flow to work - if OAuth 2.0 is not listed as an authorization option in the scenario, the Communication Arrangement will not allow you to configure it, and principal propagation will not be possible for that API.

1.3 Create a Communication System

Before creating the Communication System in S/4HANA, you first need to export the trust certificate from BTP.
 
In the SAP BTP Cockpit, navigate to Connectivity > Destinations, scroll down to the Destination Trust section, and click Export to download the certificate file. The file will be in .pem format — keep it ready for the next step.
 
In your SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud system, navigate to Communication Management > Communication Systems and create a new system. Set the Host field to your BTP subaccount subdomain.
 
Under the OAuth 2.0 Settings section, enable OAuth 2.0 Identity Provider and upload the .pem file you just exported from BTP. Once uploaded, the certificate details will be displayed — locate the CN (Common Name) field, copy its value, and paste it into the OAuth 2.0 SAML Issuer field.
 
This links S/4HANA's trust configuration to your BTP subaccount and is what allows S/4HANA to validate the SAML assertions that BTP will generate during principal propagation.
 

 

1.4 Create a Communication Arrangement

Navigate to Communication Management > Communication Arrangements, create a new arrangement, and link it to the communication scenario, communication system, and communication user created in the previous steps.
 
Note - User for inbound communication must be added with OAuth 2.0 Authentication Method. 

After save, you can see the OAuth 2.0 Details. These will be used in setting up the Destination in BTP.

Step 2 — Configure the BTP Destination

In your SAP BTP cockpit, navigate to your subaccount and open Connectivity > Destinations. Create a new destination with the following key settings:
 
Property Value
AuthenticationOAuth2SAMLBearerAssertion
AudienceYour S/4HANA system URL
Client KeyCommunication user name
Token Service URLOAuth token endpoint from the Communication Arrangement
Token Service URL Type DedicatedDedicated
Use Basic Credentials for Token Service✔️
Token Service UserCommunication user name
Token Service PasswordPassword for the Communication User created in Step 1.1
AuthnContextClassRef
urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:ac:classes:PreviousSession
Name Id Format
urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:1.1:nameid-format:emailAddress
User Id Source
email
Additional Properties
 
compactAssertion
true
 

 

 

Step 3 — Configure SAP Build Process Automation

Open your workflow in SAP Build Process Automation and locate the action step that calls the S/4HANA API.
In the step configuration, enable the option "Run on behalf of" and map it to the workflow initiator or the relevant user identity. This tells SBPA to pass the user context when invoking the destination, which, combined with the OAuth2SAMLBearerAssertion destination and the SAML trust in S/4HANA, completes the propagation chain.

How It All Fits Together

Once configured end-to-end, the flow works as follows:
  1. A user triggers the workflow in SAP Build Process Automation
  2. The action step runs on behalf of that user and calls the BTP destination
  3. BTP generates a SAML assertion carrying the user identity and exchanges it for an OAuth token from S/4HANA
  4. S/4HANA validates the token against the Communication System trust and applies its own authorization checks for that specific user

Conclusion

Principal propagation is a few configuration steps across systems, but each piece is critical. Missing any one of them, such as not uploading the correct signature in the Communication System, will cause the setup to fail entirely.
 
If you run into issues, the most useful debugging step is to test the destination directly from the BTP cockpit using Check Connection and verify in the S/4HANA application log which user identity is being received.
 
I hope this guide saves you the trial and error. Feel free to drop questions in the comments.

entry.title: From Form to Tile: Add an SAP BPA start form in SAP Build Work Zone
entry.content:
Introduction:

This guide shows how to surface an SAP Build Process Automation (SAP BPA) start form as a tile in SAP Build Work Zone, so users can trigger workflows directly from a Work Zone site. The steps apply in general, whether or not your tenant is integrated with SAP IPD Collaboration

You’ll learn how to collect the required IDs, configure the app parameters and navigation, adjust the tile’s visualization and translations, and grant role access. By the end, your site will offer a reliable, one-click entry point to launch your SAP BPA workflow.

This article provides a detailed, screenshot-rich walkthrough of the official Help documentation: see the Help page.

 

Prerequisites:

To follow this guide, you need a deployed workflow in SAP BPA with a form as the trigger and a Work Zone tenant with SAP IPD Collaboration enabled.

For more information about creating a workflow and setting up SAP Build Work Zone, please refer to the following blogs:

  • Developing Processes in SAP Build Process Automation for Integrated Product Development by @Alexandra_Rong
  • SAP Build Work Zone in Integrated Product Development: A Step-by-Step Setup Guide by @Aylinborzan

 

Step 1: Get the required information

To create a tile on your preferred Work Zone site, you need details from the SAP BPA start form. The required IDs are available as part of a link in the workflow where the form is configured as the trigger. Make sure you are viewing the deployed version of the workflow. The IDs do not change with each release cycle; they only change if you modify the trigger. Therefore, if you adjust the workflow or update fields in the form (without changing the trigger), you do not need to update the IDs in the Work Zone content settings.

In the deployed version of the workflow, click the trigger that uses the form. A details panel opens on the right. There you’ll find two links: one opens the form in a new window so you can start the workflow, and the other provides configuration details. The configuration link is required to connect to SAP Build Work Zone. Copy the link and paste it into a notes app.

 

Step 2: Check if “Process Trigger“ is already visible in “Content Manager“
